Vulnerability Path:
services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/forms/login/freemarker/FreeMarkerLoginFormsProvider.java:604-604: apply(...) : Map
services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/forms/login/freemarker/FreeMarkerLoginFormsProvider.java:611-611: attributes : Map
services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/theme/freemarker/DefaultFreeMarkerProvider.java:28-28: data : Map
services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/theme/freemarker/DefaultFreeMarkerProvider.java:49-49: data

Explaination: User-controllable attributes from a public/provider-facing mapper are passed directly into FreeMarker template rendering; if the template outputs them in HTML, this can lead to XSS without sanitization.

response.analysis: Primary FP type is B1. The reported source is misidentified: `attributeMapper` is not attacker-controlled input, but an internal server-side SPI callback exposed through `LoginFormsProvider.setAttributeMapper(...)`. In the real codebase, the production uses of this mapper only rewrite existing model beans for organization/login behavior; they do not let an external user supply arbitrary HTML-bearing attributes. The sink is also overstated: passing a map into FreeMarker is not itself XSS in Keycloak, because `.ftl` templates are rendered with `HTMLOutputFormat`, and places that intentionally allow HTML use `kcSanitize(...)?no_esc`. So the report shows a generic map-to-template path, but not a concrete user-input-to-unsafe-HTML path that is exploitable in normal Keycloak deployments. Secondary FP aspects are shallow project-level reasoning (A1) and missed escaping/sanitization context (D1).
response.evidence: `FreeMarkerLoginFormsProvider.processTemplate` applies the mapper and forwards the result to FreeMarker at `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/forms/login/freemarker/FreeMarkerLoginFormsProvider.java:604-611`. However, the mapper API itself is only an internal Java SPI method declared at `server-spi-private/src/main/java/org/keycloak/forms/login/LoginFormsProvider.java:170`. The real production call sites are internal only, in `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/organization/authentication/authenticators/browser/OrganizationAuthenticator.java:252-259`, `341-360`, `405-413`, and `435-443`; these lambdas only wrap existing beans such as `AuthenticationContextBean`, `IdentityProviderBean`, and `RealmBean`, or set `USERNAME_HIDDEN`, rather than injecting raw request data into the template model. On the rendering side, `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/theme/freemarker/DefaultFreeMarkerProvider.java:56-63` sets `cfg.setOutputFormat(HTMLOutputFormat.INSTANCE)` for `.ftl` templates, and `:28-31` injects `kcSanitize` into the model. Templates explicitly sanitize raw-HTML outputs, for example `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/template.ftl:171` and `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/error.ftl:7`. Other outputs such as `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/info.ftl:7,11`, `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/login.ftl:15`, and `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/template.ftl:134` use normal `${...}` rendering under HTML output format, which is intended to be escaped rather than emitted as raw HTML. Therefore the report does not demonstrate a real external attacker-controlled XSS path in Keycloak core.

B1.report:
Vulnerability Path:
core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/providers/anonymous/AnonymousAuthenticationToken.java:88-88: getName(...) : String
core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/AuthenticationSpringImpl.java:71-71: getName(...) : String
core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:60-60: getName(...)

Explaination: User-influenced authentication name flows into an HTTP response header, which is a header-injection/response-splitting risk rather than XSS; the source is a public getter and the sink is security-relevant.

response.analysis: The reported flow is reachable, but it is not a CWE-079 issue. `ACL.checkPermission` throws `AccessDeniedException3`, and the exception is later rendered through `reportAsHeaders`, which places `authentication.getName()` into `X-You-Are-Authenticated-As`. That is an HTTP response-header sink, not an HTML/JavaScript execution sink, so the reported path does not satisfy CWE-079. The report itself even characterizes the issue as possible header injection/response splitting rather than XSS. In the default Jenkins private security realm, usernames are also constrained by `DEFAULT_ID_REGEX = ^[\\w-]+$`, which blocks CR/LF and markup characters needed for practical header-splitting or XSS payloads during normal local account creation. Therefore this CWE-079 report is a false positive.
response.evidence: `AccessDeniedException3.reportAsHeadersImpl` uses `rsp.addHeader("X-You-Are-Authenticated-As", authentication.getName())` at `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:59-60`, so the concrete sink is an HTTP header. That method is invoked from `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedHandlerImpl.java:50-53` and `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/HudsonAuthenticationEntryPoint.java:95-99` on 403 handling. The reported source path is mostly wrapper delegation: `org/acegisecurity/providers/anonymous/AnonymousAuthenticationToken.getName()` and `org/acegisecurity/AuthenticationSpringImpl.getName()` both just delegate, and Acegi `AbstractAuthenticationToken.getName()` ultimately returns the principal username (`core/src/main/java/org/acegisecurity/providers/AbstractAuthenticationToken.java`). For the built-in local realm, username creation is validated by `containsOnlyAcceptableCharacters`, which by default enforces `DEFAULT_ID_REGEX = ^[\\w-]+$` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/HudsonPrivateSecurityRealm.java:123, 429-435, 509-513`). This means the reported path is not an XSS sink, and even the alternate header-injection interpretation is not feasible through normal local username creation.

B1.report:
Vulnerability Path:
dspace-sword/src/main/java/org/purl/sword/server/DepositServlet.java:209-209: getRemoteAddr(...) : String
dspace-sword/src/main/java/org/purl/sword/server/DepositServlet.java:212-212: filename : String
dspace-sword/src/main/java/org/purl/sword/server/DepositServlet.java:225-225: filename : String
dspace-sword/src/main/java/org/purl/sword/server/DepositServlet.java:240-240: filename : String
dspace-sword/src/main/java/org/purl/sword/base/ChecksumUtils.java:45-45: filepath : String
dspace-sword/src/main/java/org/purl/sword/base/ChecksumUtils.java:47-47: filepath

Explaination: The source is request.getRemoteAddr(), which is end-user influenced input, and it is used to form a filesystem path that is later opened via FileInputStream. Because no sanitization is assumed, attacker-controlled path content could reach file access, making this a potential path traversal issue.

response.analysis: The reported dataflow is real inside `DepositServlet.doPost()`: `filename` is built from `tempDirectory`, a fixed `"SWORD-"` prefix, `request.getRemoteAddr()`, and a counter; that path is then opened and later passed to `ChecksumUtils.generateMD5()`, which uses `FileInputStream`. However, this is not a real CWE-022 path traversal. The key problem is Type B1: `request.getRemoteAddr()` is not an arbitrary attacker-controlled path fragment. By servlet semantics it is the peer IP address string chosen by the server/network stack, so in normal operation it is constrained to IP-address syntax (IPv4 digits/dots or IPv6 hex/colons), not `../`, `/`, `\\`, or absolute paths. The control flow itself is reachable, so this is not A1/A2; the false positive comes from treating `getRemoteAddr()` as a traversal-capable source when it is not. Also, the path is anchored under the server-configured `tempDirectory`, and the later `FileInputStream` only re-opens the same temp file that was just created from the request body, not an attacker-chosen existing file.
response.evidence: `DepositServlet.init()` sets `tempDirectory` from server configuration and normalizes it with a trailing separator (`DepositServlet.java:125-133`). In `doPost()`, the file path is constructed as `filename = tempDirectory + "SWORD-" + request.getRemoteAddr() + "-" + counter.addAndGet(1);` (`DepositServlet.java:208-209`), then the upload body is written there via `new FileOutputStream(new File(filename))` (`DepositServlet.java:212`). Later, the same `filename` is passed to `ChecksumUtils.generateMD5(filename)` (`DepositServlet.java:240`), and `ChecksumUtils.generateMD5(String filepath)` does `return generateMD5(new FileInputStream(filepath));` (`ChecksumUtils.java:45-47`). So the reported path exists, but the only request-derived component is `getRemoteAddr()`. That value is a remote IP address string, not a free-form filename/header/parameter under attacker textual control, and it does not provide the path separators or traversal sequences needed for a real-world CWE-022 exploit.

A1.analysis: The reported sink is real (`new File(directory, revFilename)` during revision cleanup), but the report's source-to-sink chain is not the actual runtime behavior. In the relevant copy/overwrite/version flows, OpenOLAT versions the existing local destination file, not the SharePoint source leaf. `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.addToRevisions()` computes the revision filename from `currentLeaf` -> `currentFile` -> `currentFile.getName()`, and in the real call sites `currentLeaf` is `existingVFSItem` / `currentLeaf` (the local target), while the SharePoint item is used only as an `InputStream` supplier. Therefore the reported flow from `MicrosoftDriveItem.name()` / `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()` into `generateFilenameForRevision(...)` is infeasible for this vulnerability. Also, when a new local file is created from a source name, `LocalFolderImpl.createChildLeaf()` checks `isInPath(name)` and rejects normalized paths escaping the target container.
A1.evidence: 1) SharePoint source object: `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()` and `getName()` return `driveItem.name()`, but `DriveItemLeaf.canVersion()` returns `VFSStatus.NO`; `CopySourceLeaf.canVersion()` also returns `VFSStatus.NO`. 2) Real versioning call sites use the local destination as the first argument: in `FileCopyController`, `vfsRepositoryService.addVersion(existingVFSItem, ..., sourceLeaf.getInputStream())`; in `FolderController`, `vfsRepositoryService.addVersion(currentLeaf, ..., newLeaf.getInputStream())`. 3) In `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.addToRevisions(...)`, the code is `File currentFile = toFile(currentLeaf);` followed by `String uuid = ... generateFilenameForRevision(currentFile, ...)`; then `generateFilenameForRevision(File file, ...)` returns `generateFilenameForRevision(file.getName(), ...)`. So `revFilename` comes from the local target file name, not from the SharePoint `DriveItem` name. 4) Non-versioned copy creation is guarded: `LocalFolderImpl.createChildLeaf(String name)` calls `isInPath(name)`, and `isInPath` resolves and normalizes the child path and requires it to stay under the parent container (`normalizedPath.startsWith(bFile)`).

A1.analysis: The report identifies a mechanically possible string flow from a SharePoint filename (`MicrosoftDriveItem.name()` -> `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()`), but it does not match the real runtime dataflow into the file-opening sink. In `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl`, the path that is converted to a local `File` and later read comes from the first argument of `addVersion(...)` / `addToRevisions(...)` (`currentLeaf`), not from the replacement-content stream. In all reachable versioning workflows, that first argument is the already-existing local target file, while the SharePoint/external item is only used as `InputStream` content. So the attacker-controlled SharePoint name does not control the path passed to `VFSManager.olatRootFile()` or `FileInputStream`. Also, `DriveItemLeaf.canVersion()` is `NO`, so a SharePoint leaf is not a valid version-controlled target in normal flows. The only caller that passes `sourceLeaf` as the first argument is `CmdMoveCopy`, but that branch is internally unreachable because an earlier sanity check aborts exactly when the later branch would require `targetFile != null`. Therefore this report does not represent an exploitable CWE-22 in the real OpenOLAT workflow.
A1.evidence: 1) Sink uses the first `VFSLeaf` parameter only: `addVersion(VFSLeaf currentFile, ...)` -> `addToRevisions(currentLeaf, ...)` -> `File currentFile = toFile(currentLeaf);`, and `toFile(VFSItem item)` does `String relPath = item.getRelPath(); return relPath == null ? null : VFSManager.olatRootFile(relPath);` (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl` 1413-1435, 504-506). 2) Reachable callers pass a local target leaf, not the external source leaf: `FolderController` calls `addVersion(currentLeaf, ..., newLeaf.getInputStream())` (2442-2446); `FileCopyController` calls `addVersion(existingVFSItem, ..., sourceLeaf.getInputStream())` (231); `CourseResourceFolderWebService` calls `addVersion(existingLeaf, ..., file)` (377); `ZipUtil` calls `addVersion(newEntry, ..., oZip)` (274, 397). 3) SharePoint/external wrappers are not version targets: `DriveItemLeaf.canVersion() { return VFSStatus.NO; }` and `CopySourceLeaf.canVersion() { return VFSStatus.NO; }` (`DriveItemLeaf` 156-162; `CopySourceLeaf` 144-154). 4) The only direct `addVersion(sourceLeaf, ...)` caller is dead code: `CmdMoveCopy.doMove()` requires `targetFile != null` before calling `addVersion(sourceLeaf, ...)` (176-180), but `getSanityCheckedSourceItems()` aborts earlier when `target.resolve(vfsSource.getName()) != null` (254-257), so that branch cannot run.

A1.analysis: The reported sink is real in isolation: `PdfDeliveryDispatcher.renderFile()` builds `new File(directory, filename)` without canonical-path validation, so `filename` would be dangerous if an attacker could freely reach that call. However, in the actual OpenOLAT design, `/pdfd/` is not a normal public file endpoint. The call to `renderFile()` is gated by `PdfDelivery delivery = cache.get(key)`, and `key` must be a live, server-generated UUID that exists only during an internal PDF-rendering transaction. The report treats the whole request URI as attacker-controlled, but ignores that the first path segment is an unguessable capability token created in another class and removed immediately after rendering. As a result, an arbitrary external user cannot realistically drive a chosen `filename` into the sink, so this is a false positive caused mainly by missing project-level context.
A1.evidence: 1) In `src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/services/pdf/dispatcher/PdfDeliveryDispatcher.java`, the file read happens only after `PdfDelivery delivery = cache.get(key);` and `delivery != null`; otherwise the code returns 404. 2) In `src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/services/pdf/manager/AbstractPdfSPI.java`, every delivery key is created as `UUID.randomUUID().toString()`, stored with `cache.put(key, delivery)`, used for rendering, and then removed with `cache.remove(key)`. 3) In `src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/services/pdf/manager/GotenbergSPI.java` and `AthenaPdfSPI.java`, the `/pdfd/<key>/<rootFilename>` URL is constructed only for the configured PDF service. 4) In `src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/services/pdf/PdfControllerResource.java`, the user receives only the final PDF stream via `pdfService.convert(..., out)`; the transient `/pdfd/<key>/...` URL is not returned to the client. Therefore the report's attacker-controlled request model is incomplete: without knowing the short-lived UUID key, the attacker never reaches `renderFile()`.

A1.analysis: The reported source and sink are dangerous in isolation: `FolderCommandHelper.tryDoubleDecoding()` uses a user-influenced request path, and `VFSManager.resolveFile()` / `LocalFolderImpl.resolve()` do not sanitize `..` before building `new File(base, childName)`. However, this specific path is not exploitable in the real OpenOLAT request flow. Before any folder command runs, `UserRequestImpl.parseRequest()` derives `moduleURI` from the decoded request URI and aborts if it contains `"../"` (`UserRequestImpl.java:343-346`). `CmdServeResource` reaches `tryDoubleDecoding()` only after a `UserRequestImpl` already exists (`CmdServeResource.java:79-83`). The fallback helper was added for `+` / double-decoding filename issues; it only slices `request.getRequestURI()` and does not URL-decode it again (`FolderCommandHelper.java:76-84`). Therefore an attacker has only two cases: send a literal/decoded `../`, which is rejected before dispatch, or keep traversal percent-encoded, in which case the fallback passes `%2e%2e` literally to `resolve()` and it is treated as a filename, not parent-directory traversal. Also, the report's "absolute path" claim is overstated: `extractChild()` strips the leading slash, so the practical concern would only be `..`, not a direct absolute-path escape. Overall, the report over-approximates the dataflow and misses a mandatory project-level normalization/validation step.
A1.evidence: `UserRequestImpl.java:343-346` blocks traversal before controller execution: `moduleURI = nonParsedUri.substring(nextSlash + 1); if (moduleURI.indexOf("../") != -1) throw new AssertException(...)`. `CmdServeResource.java:79-83` shows file serving first uses `ureq.getModuleURI()` and only falls back to `FolderCommandHelper.tryDoubleDecoding(...)` after normal resolution fails. `FolderCommandHelper.java:76-84` shows the fallback only reads `request.getRequestURI()`, strips the prefix, and calls `resolve(path)`; it performs no extra percent-decoding, so encoded traversal remains encoded. `VFSManager.java:101-109` and `170-190` confirm the sink is traversal-prone only if raw `..` actually reaches it. As an adjacent real-world check, mapper-based file delivery also rejects `..` explicitly in `MapperDispatcher.java:99-103`.

A1.report:
Vulnerability Path:
activemq-tooling/activemq-joram-jms-tests/src/main/java/org/objectweb/jtests/jms/admin/AdminFactory.java:33-33: getAdminClassName(...) : String
activemq-tooling/activemq-joram-jms-tests/src/main/java/org/objectweb/jtests/jms/admin/AdminFactory.java:41-41: adminClassName

Explaination: User-controlled properties can influence the class name, which is then loaded and instantiated via Class.forName/newInstance, enabling unsafe dynamic code loading/reflection.

response.analysis: The reported sink is real reflection: AdminFactory.getAdmin() reads the property 'jms.provider.admin.class' and passes it to Class.forName(...).newInstance(). However, the report is a false positive because the source is not an attacker-reachable input in the real product. Full-project context shows this code is part of the Joram JMS test harness, not broker/runtime request handling. The only repository call to AdminFactory.getAdmin() is from JMSTestCase.setUp(), which loads properties from a classpath test resource selected by the test suite via the JVM property 'joram.jms.test.file'. Downstream modules include activemq-joram-jms-tests with Maven scope 'test' only. In practice this is a trusted extension/configuration point for local test execution, not a remotely reachable code-loading path. So the tool over-approximated the source by treating a test Properties parameter as user-controlled without tracing its concrete origin and usage context. This maps best to A1, not a real CWE-094 in deployment.
response.evidence: Dataflow in the report exists syntactically: AdminFactory.java lines 25-42 read props.getProperty('jms.provider.admin.class') and call Class.forName(adminClassName); admin = (Admin) adminClass.newInstance(). But the only caller in the repo is JMSTestCase.setUp() at lines 84-97, where props are loaded from getClass().getClassLoader().getResourceAsStream(System.getProperty('joram.jms.test.file', 'provider.properties')). The Joram suite sets that property itself: JoramJmsTest.java lines 71-83 call System.setProperty('joram.jms.test.file', 'provider.properties'). The corresponding test resource activemq-amqp/src/test/resources/provider.properties line 20 hardcodes jms.provider.admin.class=org.apache.activemq.transport.amqp.joram.ActiveMQAdmin. Maven metadata confirms test-only usage: activemq-amqp/pom.xml lines 72-78 and activemq-unit-tests/pom.xml lines 226-233 declare activemq-joram-jms-tests with <scope>test</scope>. Repository search found no non-test caller of AdminFactory.getAdmin(). Additionally, the loaded class must be on the local classpath and castable to org.objectweb.jtests.jms.admin.Admin, further showing this is an intentional test-plugin mechanism controlled by the test runner, not an attacker-controlled runtime injection path.

D4.analysis: The reported CWE-22 path is not feasible at runtime. IRIS connects a SharePoint source object (`DriveItemContainer.getRelPath()` / `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()`) to `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.toFile(...)`, but in the real code `toFile(...)` is called on the local `target` object inside `copyTo(source, target, ...)`, not on the SharePoint `source`. For the exact reported container path, `copyTo(...)` also exits immediately because `DriveItemContainer` inherits `canMeta() == NO`, so the method returns before any `File` is constructed. Even when a SharePoint leaf is copied, the local path used by `copyTo(...)` comes from the already-created local target item, whose `getRelPath()` is derived from an actual local file under the canonical root. Therefore the report does not demonstrate an attacker-controlled path escaping the repository root through the shown source-to-sink chain.
D4.evidence:
1. The reported sink is in `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.copyTo(source, target, ...)`: `File targetFile = toFile(target);` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/services/vfs/manager/VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:964-970`). The receiver is `target`, not `source`.
2. `toFile(VFSItem item)` uses `item.getRelPath()` (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:504-506`). IRIS linked this to `DriveItemContainer.getRelPath()` / `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()`, but those are SharePoint source-side implementations, not the local target object passed to `toFile(target)`.
3. For the exact reported `DriveItemContainer` path, `copyTo(...)` is unreachable because it starts with `if(source.canMeta() != VFSStatus.YES || target.canMeta() != VFSStatus.YES) return;` (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:964-966`). `DriveItemContainer` inherits `canMeta() == NO` from `AbstractVirtualContainer` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/AbstractVirtualContainer.java:151-153`), so the method returns before any file/path handling.
4. When copying into a local folder, the actual local file is chosen earlier in `LocalFolderImpl.copyFrom(...)` via `File fTarget = new File(basefile, sourcename);` and only afterwards metadata is synced using `vfsRepositoryService.copyTo(sourceLeaf, targetLeaf, this, savedBy);` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/LocalFolderImpl.java:244-256`). So the IRIS-reported `copyTo -> toFile(target)` chain is not the file-creation path from the SharePoint `getRelPath()` value.
5. The local target implementation computes its relative path only if its base file is already under the canonical root: `if(bFile.startsWith(bcRoot)) { ... return "/" + relPath; } return null;` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/LocalFileImpl.java:100-106`). This further shows that `toFile(target)` is anchored to the real local target file, not to the external SharePoint name string.

D4.analysis: Step 1: the sink `new File(root, infoMessage.getAttachmentPath())` is potentially sensitive, but the reported source is not the value that reaches it. In this workflow, `attachmentPath` is set from `storeAttachment()`, which creates or reuses a local attachment folder and returns its relative path under the info-message storage root. Step 2: the reported path is not feasible in concrete execution. IRIS linked `DriveItemLeaf.getName()` to `VFSManager.getRelativeItemPath()` through the abstract `VFSItem.getName()` call, but at this call site the runtime object is the local `attachmentFolder`, not a SharePoint `DriveItemLeaf`. The report therefore stitches together unrelated modules (SharePoint VFS items and info-message attachment storage) and ignores the concrete receiver type/object identity. Step 3: the false positive is best explained by type/structure blindness, with some shallow interprocedural reasoning as a secondary issue. Step 4: no prompt/CWE deviation is needed to explain the result.
D4.evidence: `InfoDisplayController` overwrites the field via `msg.setAttachmentPath(infoMessageManager.storeAttachment(...))` (`InfoDisplayController.java:494-500`). In `storeAttachment()`, `folderName` is either generated as `datePart + "_attachments"` or, when editing, reduced to the last segment of the old path with `String[] parts = folderName.split("/"); folderName = parts[parts.length - 1];`; then a local folder is obtained with `VFSManager.getOrCreateContainer(ressourceContainer, folderName)` and the stored value is `VFSManager.getRelativeItemPath(attachmentFolder, getStoragePath(), null)` (`InfoMessageFrontendManagerImpl.java:163-188`). Thus `attachmentPath` is a system-generated local folder path, not a SharePoint filename. The alleged source enters `VFSManager.getRelativeItemPath()` only via the generic `tmpItem.getName()` call (`VFSManager.java:545-558`), but here `tmpItem` is the local `attachmentFolder` created in `storeAttachment()`, not `DriveItemLeaf`. Additional hardening exists for stored attachment leaf names: `LocalFolderImpl.createChildLeaf()` rejects names whose normalized path would escape the parent by checking `isInPath(name)` (`LocalFolderImpl.java:480-485`, `529-537`). Also, `InfoEditFormController` only carries forward the existing `attachmentPath` (`InfoEditFormController.java:109`, `317-320`); there is no code path in this workflow that assigns a SharePoint-controlled `DriveItem.getName()` value to `attachmentPath`.

D4.analysis: The reported sink at `InfoMessageFrontendManagerImpl.java:267-274` (`new File(root, infoMessage.getAttachmentPath())`) would only be a CWE-022 issue if `attachmentPath` were attacker-controlled. In the real workflow, it is not derived from SharePoint `DriveItem.getName()`. `attachmentPath` is set in `InfoDisplayController.java:495-500` only from `storeAttachment()`, and `storeAttachment()` creates/uses a folder under the fixed `/infomessages/` storage tree and returns an application-generated path for that local folder. The IRIS path is not runtime-feasible: it followed the generic `VFSItem.getName()` call inside `VFSManager.getRelativeItemPath()` and treated it as if the runtime receiver were `DriveItemLeaf`, but at this call site the receiver is a local `LocalFolderImpl` produced by `getResourceContainer()`. There is no concrete call/data-flow from the SharePoint classes into this info-message attachment path. Additionally, the real attachment creation path strips any existing folder path to its basename and the local VFS layer rejects names escaping the parent directory via normalized `isInPath()` checks. Therefore this report is a false positive, primarily due to type/receiver confusion.
D4.evidence: `storeAttachment()` uses `getResourceContainer(ores)` and the fixed storage root `VFSManager.olatRootContainer("/infomessages/", null)` (`InfoMessageFrontendManagerImpl.java:207-232`). If `folderName` already exists, it is reduced to its last path segment only (`InfoMessageFrontendManagerImpl.java:171-177`), then the folder is created/resolved with `VFSManager.getOrCreateContainer(...)` (`line 179`), and the stored path is generated from that local folder via `VFSManager.getRelativeItemPath(...)` (`line 188`). The runtime object there is a `LocalFolderImpl`, whose `getName()` is `basefile.getName()` (`LocalImpl.java:107-108`), not SharePoint `DriveItemLeaf.getName()` (`DriveItemLeaf.java:127-128`). The VFS layer also blocks traversal during creation: `LocalFolderImpl.isInPath()` normalizes the candidate path and requires it to stay under the parent (`LocalFolderImpl.java:480-484`), and both `createChildContainer()` and `createChildLeaf()` abort when `!isInPath(name)` (`LocalFolderImpl.java:513-519`, `530-536`). The only place `attachmentPath` is freshly assigned in this feature is `InfoDisplayController.java:495-500`; the REST endpoint shown in `InfoMessagesWebService.java:130-160` does not accept or set attachment paths. So the SharePoint filename source is unrelated to the sink in reachable code.

D4.analysis: The reported sink is real in isolation, but this end-to-end CWE-022 path is not exploitable. IRIS over-approximates two different object lifecycles: external SharePoint items and local OpenOlat revision files. More importantly, the value that reaches the deletion sink is not the original SharePoint path string. Before a revision filename is stored, the code converts the `File` to `file.getName()`, which keeps only the last path component. So traversal/path prefixes from the SharePoint item name do not flow into `fileToDelete`. In normal application logic, SharePoint leaves are also not versionable local VFS files (`canVersion() == NO`), while revision deletion operates on persisted local `VFSMetadataImpl`/`VFSRevisionImpl` objects.
D4.evidence: Source side: `MicrosoftDriveItem.name()` returns `driveItem.getName()` (MicrosoftDriveItem.java:37-38), and `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()` returns that name (DriveItemLeaf.java:87-88). The local conversion step `toFile(VFSItem)` uses `VFSManager.olatRootFile(relPath)` (VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:504-506), which assumes a local bcroot-relative path even though SharePoint items are external. The crucial break is in revision-name generation: `generateFilenameForRevision(File file, ...)` immediately calls `generateFilenameForRevision(file.getName(), ...)` (VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:1985-1986). `File.getName()` is only the basename, so path separators / traversal structure do not propagate into the stored revision filename. Later deletion uses that stored basename-derived value: `String fileToDelete = versionImpl.getFilename(); ... new File(directory, fileToDelete); Files.deleteIfExists(...)` (VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:1561-1587). Also, SharePoint leaves are not normal versionable local files: `DriveItemLeaf.canVersion()` returns `VFSStatus.NO` (DriveItemLeaf.java:155-157); SharePoint metadata is transient (`DriveItemMetadata extends VFSTransientMetadata`); but revisions are persisted against `@ManyToOne(targetEntity=VFSMetadataImpl.class, optional=false)` in `VFSRevisionImpl` (VFSRevisionImpl.java:118-126). Normal versioning call sites also gate on versionable local targets, e.g. FileUploadController.java:500 and FolderController.java:2442-2446. Therefore an attacker-controlled SharePoint item name does not realistically become an arbitrary path deleted by `deleteRevisions()`.

response.analysis: The reported path is a false positive. IRIS correctly sees that `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()` returns the remote SharePoint/OneDrive item name (`driveItem.name()`), and that `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.toFile(VFSItem)` would convert an arbitrary `getRelPath()` into `VFSManager.olatRootFile(relPath)`. However, the report misses the real runtime semantics of this type. `DriveItemLeaf` is a `VFSExternalLeaf`/`VFSExternalItem`, and `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.getMetadataFor(VFSItem)` has a dedicated branch for external items that returns `externalItem.getMetaInfo()` directly instead of calling `toFile()` on them. In the normal design, SharePoint-backed items are handled as external objects, not as local files under `bcroot`. In addition, nearly all versioning/edit paths that call into `addVersion()` are gated by `canVersion() == YES`; `DriveItemLeaf.canVersion()` is hardcoded `NO`, so those paths do not normally accept SharePoint leaves. The tool therefore stitched together a cross-type path from an external VFS item into a local-file sink without respecting the external-item control flow and type split.
response.evidence: Source: `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()` returns `driveItem.name()` (`DriveItemLeaf.java:87-88`). But `DriveItemLeaf` is an external type and is non-versionable/read-only: `canWrite() == NO` (`DriveItemLeaf.java:146-147`), `canVersion() == NO` (`DriveItemLeaf.java:156-157`). The repository service explicitly special-cases external items: `if(path instanceof VFSExternalItem externalItem) { return externalItem.getMetaInfo(); }` before any `toFile()` call (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:345-351`). This defeats the report's main external-to-local sink path for normal metadata handling. The claimed sink conversion exists (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.toFile(VFSItem)` -> `VFSManager.olatRootFile(relPath)` at `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:504-506`, `VFSManager.java:90-92`), but it is not the standard path used for `DriveItemLeaf`. Real callers also usually require versionable/local leaves before calling `addVersion()`, e.g. WebDAV only versions when `childLeaf.canVersion() == YES` (`VFSResourceRoot.java:180-182`), and file-copy overwrite uses the target existing local item, not the external source, for `addVersion()` (`FileCopyController.java:230-231`). There is one suspicious separate bug in `CmdMoveCopy` that calls `addVersion(sourceLeaf, ...)` on the source when the target is versioned, but that is not the path reported by IRIS and does not change that the reported path itself ignores the `VFSExternalItem` type split.

D1.analysis: The reported dataflow is syntactically present, but it is not an exploitable CWE-79 in the real runtime path. In the template path, the async job id is constructed from internal template metadata and context entries, then each id segment is URL-encoded twice before being written into the placeholder HTML. That encoding neutralizes the characters needed for HTML/attribute breakout (`"`, `'`, `<`, `>`, space, `=`, `&`, `/`, `\`) into `%..` or `+` sequences. On the client side, the value is not executed as HTML or script; `async.js` reads `data-xwiki-async-id` via `dataset` and only appends it to an AJAX URL (`/asyncrenderer/` + id). So even if an attacker could influence some id component, the resulting output is an opaque fetch token, not executable markup. This makes the report a false positive. The main failure mode is missed sanitization/contextual encoding rather than a real XSS sink.
D1.evidence: 1) Template flow source: `TemplateAsyncRenderer.initialize()` sets `this.id = createId("template", template.getId(), this.targetSyntax.toIdString(), inline);` so the reported path is based on an async job identifier, not raw HTML content ([xwiki-platform-core/xwiki-platform-oldcore/src/main/java/com/xpn/xwiki/internal/template/TemplateAsyncRenderer.java:78-89]). 2) Sanitization on the path: `AsyncRendererExecutorResponse.getJobIdHTTPPath()` applies `URLEncoder.encode(element, "UTF8")` twice to every id segment before appending it to the output ([xwiki-platform-core/xwiki-platform-rendering/xwiki-platform-rendering-async/xwiki-platform-rendering-async-api/src/main/java/org/xwiki/rendering/async/internal/AsyncRendererExecutorResponse.java:82-112]). 3) Test evidence confirms escaping: `value1\` becomes `value1%25255c` and `value2/` becomes `value2%25252f` in the emitted HTTP path ([xwiki-platform-core/xwiki-platform-rendering/xwiki-platform-rendering-async/xwiki-platform-rendering-async-default/src/test/java/org/xwiki/rendering/async/internal/DefaultAsyncRendererExecutorTest.java:149-172]). 4) Client-side use is non-executable: `async.js` reads `element.dataset.xwikiAsyncId` and builds `url = "${request.contextPath}/asyncrenderer/" + id;`, then performs `$.get({ url: url, ... })`; it does not inject the id into `innerHTML` or evaluate it as script ([xwiki-platform-core/xwiki-platform-web/xwiki-platform-web-war/src/main/webapp/resources/uicomponents/async/async.js:62-85]). 5) The exact string placeholder builder is also malformed as HTML (`class=xwiki-async` is immediately followed by `data-xwiki-async-id=...` without a separating space), which further weakens the claimed HTML-attribute injection model ([xwiki-platform-core/xwiki-platform-rendering/xwiki-platform-rendering-async/xwiki-platform-rendering-async-api/src/main/java/org/xwiki/rendering/async/internal/block/DefaultBlockAsyncRendererExecutor.java:163-176]).

D1.analysis: The reported data flow exists, but it is not a practical CWE-022 in DSpace as implemented. `auth.getUsername()` is indeed carried into `filenameBase`, and that value is passed to `new File(path, filenameBase)` and then `FileOutputStream`. However, the tool missed an important semantic detail: the attacker-controlled value is always prefixed with `"sword-"`. Because of that prefix, the child path can never be absolute, and a raw `../` at the start of the username does not become a parent-directory segment. Traversal would only be possible if the username contains an internal `/../...` sequence and the first path segment (`sword-<prefix>`) already exists as a directory under `failed-package.dir`. This code never creates those intermediate directories, so in normal operation the write either stays inside the configured directory or fails with `IOException`. That exception is caught by callers and only logged during failed-ingest handling. The path is also only reached when `swordv2-server.keep-package-on-fail` is enabled. So the report overstates a string-concatenation issue into a real-world path traversal vulnerability.
D1.evidence:
1. Sink construction in `dspace-swordv2/src/main/java/org/dspace/sword2/DSpaceSwordAPI.java`:
`String filenameBase = "sword-" + auth.getUsername() + "-" + Instant.now().toEpochMilli();`
`File packageFile = new File(path, filenameBase);`
`new FileOutputStream(packageFile);`
This hardcoded `"sword-"` prefix means the child path is never absolute.
2. The same methods only verify the base directory exists:
`if (!dir.exists() || !dir.isDirectory()) { throw new IOException(...); }`
There is no `mkdir()`/`mkdirs()` for attacker-controlled intermediate segments.
3. Example: username `a/../../etc/passwd@example.com` produces a path like `failedPackageDir/sword-a/../../etc/passwd@example.com-<ts>`. This only escapes if `failedPackageDir/sword-a` already exists as a directory; otherwise `FileOutputStream` fails because parent directories are missing.
4. The callers swallow that failure as a warning during error handling, e.g. `CollectionDepositManagerDSpace` and `MediaResourceManagerDSpace` wrap `storePackageAsFile()` / `storeEntryAsFile()` in `try { ... } catch (IOException e2) { log.warn(...); }`, then rethrow the original ingest exception. So a bad username normally does not create an arbitrary file and does not add a new crash path.
5. This logic is only enabled when failed-package preservation is configured: `SwordConfigurationDSpace` reads `swordv2-server.keep-package-on-fail`, and the sample config comments it out (`#swordv2-server.keep-package-on-fail=false`, `#swordv2-server.failed-package.dir=${dspace.dir}/upload`).

D1.analysis: The reported sink is real, but the specific IRIS path is not feasible as described. `VFSManager.resolveFile()` indeed does not sanitize `..`, but IRIS started its path too late in the request lifecycle. Before `CmdServeResource` or `FolderCommandHelper.tryDoubleDecoding()` can use the path, `UserRequestImpl.parseRequest()` decodes the request and rejects any module URI containing `"../"`. Therefore the traversal payload described in the report cannot reach the reported file-serving sink through this path. This is a false positive caused by missing an earlier framework validation point. Separately, there appears to be a different sibling issue for bare `".."` in the browse flow, but that is outside the reported path.
D1.evidence:
1) Early guard omitted by IRIS: `UserRequestImpl.parseRequest()` sets `moduleURI = nonParsedUri.substring(nextSlash + 1);` and then checks `if (moduleURI.indexOf("../") != -1) throw new AssertException(...)` at `src/main/java/org/olat/core/gui/UserRequestImpl.java:343-347`. Any `../` traversal string is stopped before folder commands execute.
2) The reported helper is only reached later: `CmdServeResource.execute()` first resolves `ureq.getModuleURI()` and only calls `FolderCommandHelper.tryDoubleDecoding()` as a fallback if that returned null (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/modules/bc/commands/CmdServeResource.java:79-84`). So the attack string must first survive `UserRequestImpl`, which it does not.
3) `FolderCommandHelper.tryDoubleDecoding()` is explicitly a double-decoding workaround for `+` handling, not the primary request parser (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/modules/bc/commands/FolderCommandHelper.java:76-84`).
4) The sink logic is dangerous in isolation: `sanitizePath()` only normalizes slashes (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/VFSManager.java:101-109`) and `resolveFile()` does `new File(base, childName)` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/VFSManager.java:187-193`). But for the reported `../` file-serving path, the earlier parser check prevents exploitability.

response.analysis: The reported HTTP-body-to-`XStream.fromXML` flow is reachable, but the report's key claim is wrong for this codebase: this is not `fromXML` on an unrestricted XStream instance. `HttpTunnelServlet.doPost()` reads the request body and passes it to `wireFormat.unmarshalText(...)`, and `XStreamWireFormat.unmarshalText(Reader)` does call `getXStream().fromXML(reader)`. However, `XStreamWireFormat.createXStream()` builds that parser through `XStreamSupport.createXStream()`, which installs `NoTypePermission.NONE` and then a strict allowlist. By default, only primitives, arrays, `Collection`, `Map`, `String`, and classes under trusted package patterns are allowed; arbitrary attacker-chosen gadget classes are not. That missed hardening is the decisive reason this is a false positive. The `Command` cast in `HttpTunnelServlet` also means non-`Command` payloads do not reach broker command handling. In real deployments, this path may reject malformed/forbidden payloads or return an error for a request, but it is not a default CWE-094 code-execution issue. A materially different risk would exist only if an administrator explicitly disabled the restriction by setting `-Dorg.apache.activemq.SERIALIZABLE_PACKAGES=*`, which is an unsafe non-default override rather than the product's normal behavior.
response.evidence: Reachable path: `activemq-http/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/transport/http/HttpTunnelServlet.java:137-144` reads `request.getInputStream()` and executes `Command command = (Command) wireFormat.unmarshalText(new InputStreamReader(stream, "UTF-8"));`. `activemq-http/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/transport/xstream/XStreamWireFormat.java:69-70` then does `Object val = getXStream().fromXML(reader);`. Hardening that the report missed: `activemq-http/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/transport/xstream/XStreamWireFormat.java:125-127` creates XStream via `final XStream xstream = XStreamSupport.createXStream();`. `activemq-client/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/util/XStreamSupport.java:32-44` configures `stream.addPermission(NoTypePermission.NONE);`, adds only primitive/array/`Collection`/`Map`/`String` permissions, and allows wildcard types only for trusted packages. `activemq-client/src/main/java/org/apache/activemq/util/ClassLoadingAwareObjectInputStream.java:42-43` sets the default trusted packages to `java.lang,org.apache.activemq,org.fusesource.hawtbuf,com.thoughtworks.xstream.mapper`, and `:92-93` shows `AnyTypePermission.ANY` is enabled only when the property is explicitly set to `*`. Post-deserialization constraint: `HttpTunnelServlet.java:144-165` casts the result to `Command` and only then calls `transport.doConsume(command)`; forbidden or non-`Command` objects fail before normal broker processing. The only `TransientInitializer` hooks on this path are benign field resets (`ConsumerInfo.initTransients`, `MessagePull.initTransients`, `ActiveMQObjectMessage.initTransients`), not execution primitives.
response.new_reason:: Secondary issue: the report is also misaligned with CWE-094. This code performs XML object deserialization, not code generation/evaluation; if the allowlist were absent, the issue would be closer to unsafe deserialization (for example CWE-502) than CWE-094.

response.analysis: The reported source and sink are both real in isolation, but the claimed CWE-022 path is not exploitable in the actual execution path. The request-derived mapper suffix is filtered in `MapperDispatcher.execute()` before `LibraryThumbnailMapper.handle()` is invoked: any `mod` containing `..` is rejected with HTTP 403. `DispatcherModule.subtractContextPath()` also URL-decodes the URI when needed, so encoded traversal payloads such as `%2e%2e` are normalized before that check. After this guard, `LibraryThumbnailMapper` only accepts paths under a fixed library shared-folder prefix, so the flow does not let an attacker traverse to arbitrary local files. This is therefore a false positive caused by missing a key sanitizing program point on the reported path.
response.evidence:
1. Exact sanitizer on the reported path: in `src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/mapper/MapperDispatcher.java:105-109`, the code does `String mod = slashPos > 0 ? subInfo.substring(slashPos) : ""; if (mod.indexOf("..") != -1) { ... hres.setStatus(HttpServletResponse.SC_FORBIDDEN); return; }`. `LibraryThumbnailMapper.handle(mod, hreq)` is only called afterward at line 112, so `..` never reaches the file sink.
2. Encoded traversal is also blocked: `src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/DispatcherModule.java:126-132` performs `requestUri = URLDecoder.decode(requestUri, UTF8_ENCODING);` before the mapper path is checked, so `%2e%2e` becomes `..` and is rejected.
3. The mapper is additionally scoped to the library shared folder: `src/main/java/org/olat/modules/library/ui/LibraryThumbnailMapper.java:54-57` only proceeds when `relPath.startsWith(basePath)`. That `basePath` is built in `src/main/java/org/olat/modules/library/ui/LibraryMainController.java:176-183` as `/repository/<resId>/` plus the shared-folder name, and the actual shared-folder directory name comes from the constant `FOLDER_NAME = "_sharedfolder_"` in `src/main/java/org/olat/modules/sharedfolder/SharedFolderManager.java:71,95-99`.
4. The sink `src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/VFSManager.java:65-67` is `new File(FolderConfig.getCanonicalRoot() + fileRelPath)`, but because the dispatcher strips traversal inputs before this point, the reported request-to-file path does not allow arbitrary filesystem escape in practice.

Explaination: User-controlled request URI flows into file path construction and is later used as a local File for thumbnail generation without shown validation against absolute or '..' traversal, so this can enable path traversal.

D2.analysis: The source and sink are real, but the reported CWE-022 is not exploitable in this code path. `request.getPathInfo()` is user-controlled and reaches `new File(...)`, so B1 does not apply. The problem is the tool misinterprets the code semantics: `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` is not a weak/no-op sanitizer, because it canonicalizes `//`, `/./`, resolves `/../`, and returns `null` when traversal would escape the logical root. `StaticServlet` checks that `null` and rejects the request. In addition, the report appears to misunderstand Java `File(File,String)` behavior in this codebase: a child path beginning with `/` does not escape the configured parent root here, so the constructed `File` stays under the intended static/customizing directory. This is therefore not an A1/A2 reachability issue, and not a C1 prompt issue; it is primarily a code-semantics error (D2), with a missed sanitizer as additional context.
D2.evidence:
1) Public entry point: `src/main/webapp-tomcat/WEB-INF/web.xml:135-138` maps `rawservlet` to `/raw/*`, so `StaticServlet.doGet()` is reachable from external requests.
2) Reported path construction: `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:100-108` takes `/raw/static/...`, extracts `staticRelPath`, normalizes it, and builds `File file = new File(folderManager.getRootFile(), normalizedRelPath);`.
3) Actual sanitizer: `src/main/java/org/olat/core/gui/media/ServletUtil.java:667-692` replaces backslashes, adds a leading `/`, collapses `//`, removes `/./`, and resolves `/../`. Crucially, at lines 687-690 it does `if (index == 0) return (null); // Trying to go outside our context`. Therefore inputs like `../../etc/passwd` normalize to `null`, and `StaticServlet` rejects them at `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:102-105`.
4) Java/File semantics invalidate the claimed absolute-path escape. The project itself relies on this exact constructor pattern in `src/main/java/org/olat/admin/sysinfo/manager/CustomStaticFolderManager.java:49-69`, where `STATIC_FOLDER = "/customizing/static/"` and `getRootFile()` returns `new File(WebappHelper.getUserDataRoot(), STATIC_FOLDER)`. If a leading `/` in the child escaped the parent, this manager would point to filesystem root `/customizing/static/`, which is not the intended design.
5) Runtime confirmation in this environment: a JShell check printed `new File(new File("/base/root"), "/etc/passwd").getPath()` as `/base/root/etc/passwd`, not `/etc/passwd`. So even an input that normalizes to `/etc/passwd` remains under the configured root directory rather than escaping it.
6) Additional safeguard: if an odd path such as `/raw/static/a/..` survives normalization as a directory reference, `StaticServlet` blocks directories with `file.isDirectory()` and sends forbidden at `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:109-114`.

D2.analysis: Step 1 (B1): `request.getPathInfo()` is user-controlled and the later file open/read is a real sink, so the source/sink choice itself is not the main problem. Step 2 (A1/A2): the `/raw/*` servlet path is reachable and the reported control flow is not obviously impossible. Step 3 (D2): the reported CWE-022 is not exploitable because the path is normalized and kept under the static-resource root. `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` removes `.`/`..` segments and returns `null` if traversal would escape above the context root. For remaining leading-slash paths, the Java `File(parent, child)` constructor on the Unix JDK used here resolves the child under the parent rather than treating it as an arbitrary absolute filesystem path, so `/etc/passwd` becomes `<webapp>/static/etc/passwd`, not `/etc/passwd`. The fallback branch also re-anchors with `getContextRealPath("/static" + fallbackPath)`, so it still stays inside `/static`. Step 4 (C1): no prompt/CWE-definition deviation is needed to explain this result; the false alarm comes from misreading Java path-construction semantics.
D2.evidence: `StaticServlet.java:91-94` and `125-128` normalize the request-derived path and reject it when normalization returns `null`. `ServletUtil.java:668-691` replaces backslashes, forces a leading `/`, collapses `//` and `/./`, resolves `/../`, and explicitly returns `null` when traversal would go above root (`if (index == 0) return null`). The main sink is `StaticServlet.java:156`: `new File(staticAbsPath, normalizedRelPath)`. In the JDK used here, `java.io.File` (`File.java:320-327`) delegates to `FS.resolve(parent, child)`, and `UnixFileSystem.java:113-120` shows that when `child` starts with `/`, it returns `parent + child` (or the special `/` case), not `child` alone. Therefore a request like `/raw/_noversion_/etc/passwd` resolves to `<context>/static/etc/passwd`, not the host file `/etc/passwd`. The fallback path is also anchored: `StaticServlet.java:172-176` does `fallbackPath = ServletUtil.normalizePath(fallbackPath);` then `WebappHelper.getContextRealPath(STATIC_DIR_NAME + fallbackPath);` before creating `new File(fallbackAbsPath)`. A traversal payload such as `/raw/_noversion_/../../etc/passwd` is blocked because `normalizePath()` returns `null`, after which the servlet sends not found.

D2.analysis: B1: the source and sink are real: `HttpServletRequest.getPathInfo()` is user-controlled and the resulting `File` is later accessed through real filesystem operations (`exists()`, `isDirectory()`, `file.length()`, `FileInputStream`). A1/A2: the control flow is reachable in normal use because `StaticServlet` is mapped to `/raw/*`, and `doGet()` passes the normalized path into `new File(...)`. However, this is still a false positive because the report’s exploitability claim depends on a wrong interpretation of Java path construction semantics. The key issue is D2 (poor language-feature support): `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` always returns a path starting with `/`, and the report assumes this makes `new File(root, normalizedRelPath)` escape `root`. That assumption is wrong for Java `File(File,String)`: OpenJDK `File.java` states that if the child path is absolute, it is converted into a relative pathname in a system-dependent way, and `UnixFileSystem.resolve()` resolves a child starting with `/` as `parent + child` when the parent is not `/`. So `new File(staticAbsPath, "/etc/passwd")` becomes `<staticAbsPath>/etc/passwd`, not `/etc/passwd`. The same constructor contract prevents the reported escape in practice. In addition, classic traversal attempts containing `/../` are rejected by `normalizePath()` when they would cross the root (`index == 0` returns `null`). Trailing `/..` is not fully canonicalized, but those cases resolve to directories like `<root>/..`; both branches check `isDirectory()` and return `403`, so they still do not produce arbitrary file reads. Therefore the reported path reaches a filesystem sink, but not a CWE-022 path traversal outside the intended static/customizing roots.
D2.evidence: Reachable flow: `src/main/webapp-tomcat/WEB-INF/web.xml` maps `rawservlet` to `/raw/*` (lines 91-94, 135-137). In `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java`, `doGet()` reads `request.getPathInfo()` (line 79), extracts a relative path (lines 101-102 / 125-126), normalizes it, then constructs a file with `new File(folderManager.getRootFile(), normalizedRelPath)` (line 108) or `new File(staticAbsPath, normalizedRelPath)` (line 156). The sink is real: `FileMediaResource.getSize()` returns `file.length()` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/gui/media/FileMediaResource.java`, lines 118-120), and `deliverFile()` also uses `new FileInputStream(file)` (`StaticServlet.java`, lines 193-196 in the continuation). Sanitization: `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` prepends a leading `/` if needed (line 669) and rejects traversal when `/../` reaches the beginning (`if (index == 0) return null;`, lines 685-691). Crucial Java semantics: OpenJDK `java.io.File` says an absolute child is converted to a relative pathname in a system-dependent way, then resolved against the parent (`File.java`, lines 343-352, 359-370 from the local JDK source). OpenJDK `UnixFileSystem.resolve()` shows that when `child.charAt(0) == '/'` and `parent` is not `/`, it returns `parent + child` (`UnixFileSystem.java`, lines 113-120 from the local JDK source). Therefore a payload like `/raw/static/../../etc/passwd` is rejected by `normalizePath()`, and a path that merely starts with `/` does not escape the configured root when passed to `new File(parent, child)`.

D2.analysis: The report follows a real user-controlled source (`request.getPathInfo()`), but the claimed CWE-22 is not actually exploitable in this code path. `StaticServlet#doGet` normalizes all request-derived paths with `ServletUtil.normalizePath(...)` before file access. That helper removes `//` and `/./`, collapses internal `/../`, and returns `null` when traversal would escape the allowed root. More importantly, the reported sink semantics are misread: `deliverStatic` uses `new File(staticAbsPath, normalizedRelPath)`, and in Java this constructor resolves the child against the parent instead of treating a slash-prefixed child as a direct absolute-path escape. So a request like `/raw/_noversion_/etc/passwd` resolves under the application's `static` directory, not to `/etc/passwd`. The fallback branch is also rooted under `/static` by construction. As a result, the shown path does not lead to arbitrary file read in real deployments.
D2.evidence: 1) Request path is normalized before file construction: `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:91-93`, `101-103`, and `125-128` all call `ServletUtil.normalizePath(...)` on request-derived data. 2) The sanitizer rejects upward traversal: `src/main/java/org/olat/core/gui/media/ServletUtil.java:667-691` adds a leading slash, normalizes separators, removes `//` and `/./`, and returns `null` if `/../` would escape above the root (`index == 0`). Therefore inputs such as `../x` or `/foo/../../x` are rejected before any file read. 3) The core false-positive reason is Java path-construction semantics. `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:156` does `new File(staticAbsPath, normalizedRelPath)`. In the local JDK source, `java.io.File(File parent, String child)` calls `FS.resolve(parent.path, FS.normalize(child))`; `java.io.UnixFileSystem.resolve` concatenates a slash-prefixed child to the parent instead of discarding the parent, and OpenJDK's Windows `WinNTFileSystem.resolve` likewise drops the slash prefix before appending to the parent. So `/etc/passwd` becomes `<staticAbsPath>/etc/passwd` (or the Windows equivalent under the static root), not an arbitrary filesystem path. 4) The fallback path also stays rooted: `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:172-179` computes `fallbackPath = normalizePath(...)` and then resolves `STATIC_DIR_NAME + fallbackPath` via `getContextRealPath(...)`, so the servlet context only looks under `/static/...`. 5) Non-file outcomes are blocked: if the target is a directory, `StaticServlet.java:192-194` returns 403 instead of reading it. Even the imperfect terminal `/..` case does not yield arbitrary file disclosure.

D3.analysis: The reported dataflow is only partially correct. `MetadataUtilities.readContentsFile()` and `ContentsEntry.parse()` do place the first token from the `contents` file into `ce.filename`, and `AddBitstreamsAction.addBitstream()` later builds `new File(dir, ce.filename)` and opens it. However, before any call to `addBitstream()`, `AddBitstreamsAction.execute()` enumerates the actual direct child files of `dir` and rejects every `ContentsEntry` whose `ce.filename` is not exactly one of those names. Because this check is performed on the concrete filename value before the sink, traversal payloads such as `../etc/passwd`, `/etc/passwd`, `subdir/file`, or `C:\...` cannot pass validation and instead raise an exception. So the attacker-controlled values needed for CWE-022 do not reach the sink at runtime. The component is also a local batch CLI (`ItemUpdate`) that operates on a filesystem archive prepared for administrative use, not a network-facing request path. The decisive false-positive reason is therefore that the analysis ignored a value-constraining guard and reported an infeasible exploit path.
D3.evidence: The source is real: `ContentsEntry.parse()` assigns `arp[0]` to the filename field (`ContentsEntry.java:68-69`) and returns `new ContentsEntry(arp[0], ...)` (`ContentsEntry.java:105`). The sink is also real: `addBitstream()` does `File f = new File(dir, ce.filename);` (`AddBitstreamsAction.java:136`) and then `new FileInputStream(f)` (`AddBitstreamsAction.java:174`). But the missing guard is in `AddBitstreamsAction.execute()`: it first gets `String[] files = dir.list(ItemUpdate.fileFilter);` (`AddBitstreamsAction.java:80`), builds `fileList` from those returned names (`AddBitstreamsAction.java:81-85`), and then rejects any entry not present with `if (!fileList.contains(ce.filename)) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("File listed in contents is missing: " + ce.filename); }` (`AddBitstreamsAction.java:87-90`). `fileFilter` only accepts actual files under that directory (`ItemUpdate.java:107-112`). Since `dir.list(...)` returns immediate directory-entry names, path-traversal strings are not members of that set and cannot reach `FileInputStream`. The surrounding per-item processing catches the exception (`ItemUpdate.java:412-417,429-431`) rather than reading an arbitrary path.
D3.new_reason:: A symlink inside the archive directory could theoretically point outside the directory, but that would be a link-following issue closer to CWE-59, not the reported CWE-022 path-traversal via `..` or absolute paths.

D3.analysis: The taint source is real but the reported CWE-79 sink is not exploitable in XWiki's actual request flow. `AbstractRequestParameterConverter.convert()` reads attacker-controlled `RequiresConversion` / `RequiresHTMLConversion` values, and the converters store each chosen field name as the key in `conversionResult.getErrors()`. However, the only place where that key is reflected into the HTTP response is the AJAX-only branch in `handleConversionErrors()`: `sendError(400, ...)` runs only when `X-Requested-With == XMLHttpRequest`. In the normal WYSIWYG save flow, XWiki submits a regular form to the `action` servlet, so this branch is not taken; instead the code stores the errors in session and redirects. I also did not find any later project code that renders `com.xpn.xwiki.wysiwyg.server.converter.errors` into HTML. So the report over-approximates the runtime path and treats an AJAX error path as a browser XSS sink. Additionally, the report explanation is partly inaccurate: for HTML conversion failures, `DefaultHTMLConverter.fromHTML()` rethrows a constant `RuntimeException("Exception while parsing HTML", e)`, so user content is not reflected through the exception message in the shown path.
D3.evidence:
1. Source/key flow is real: `AbstractRequestParameterConverter.convert()` reads `request.getParameterValues(getConverterParameterName())`; `DefaultRequestParameterConverter` / `HTMLRequestParameterConverter` iterate `for (String parameterName : parametersRequiringConversion)` and on failure do `conversionResult.getErrors().put(parameterName, e)`.
2. The reflected sink is guarded by a path condition: in `AbstractRequestParameterConverter.handleConversionErrors()`, `sendError(400, errorMessage...)` is executed only inside `if (originalRequest instanceof HttpServletRequest httpRequest && "XMLHttpRequest".equals(httpRequest.getHeader("X-Requested-With")))`; otherwise the code saves the errors in session (`CONVERSION_ERRORS`) and performs a redirect.
3. Real XWiki usage is non-AJAX here: `web.xml` maps `ConversionFilter` only to the `action` servlet, and `xwiki-platform-ckeditor-plugins/.../xwiki-save/plugin.js` updates hidden fields and triggers normal form submission via action buttons; it does not send an XHR.
4. Repo search for `com.xpn.xwiki.wysiwyg.server.converter.errors` found only the producer in `AbstractRequestParameterConverter`; no later HTML renderer of these stored errors was found.
5. The report's explanation about exception messages is weak: `DefaultHTMLConverter.fromHTML()` catches any exception and throws `new RuntimeException("Exception while parsing HTML", e)`, i.e. a constant message, not reflected user HTML content.

response.analysis: This is a false positive for CWE-022. The reported condition `path.contains("")` is indeed always true, but its actual effect is to make the normalization/traversal check unconditional, not to disable it. In `ClassPathResource.isInvalidPath`, the code always executes `StringUtils.cleanPath(path)` and then rejects cleaned paths containing `../`. In the real Spark request flow, user-controlled static-file paths are also canonicalized before `ClassPathResource` is created, and the resolved resource is then checked to ensure it still stays under the configured static root. So the report identifies a strange/dead condition, but not an exploitable path traversal. The tool missed the key sanitization/check points in the surrounding code path and treated a speculative missing null-byte check as a real traversal primitive.
response.evidence:
1) `ClassPathResource.isInvalidPath` does not become permissive because of `path.contains("")`; it becomes unconditional: lines 104-107 do `path = StringUtils.cleanPath(path); if (path.contains("../")) return true;`.
2) The only repository-reachable attacker-controlled path into `ClassPathResource` is static-file serving. `ClassPathResourceHandler.getResource(String)` first does `path = UriPath.canonical(path);` before `new ClassPathResource(addedPath);` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java`, lines 67-71).
3) `UriPath.canonical` removes `.` / `..` segments and returns `null` when traversal would escape root (`src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java`). The tests confirm this behavior, e.g. `"/../foo" -> null` and `"/foo/../../bar" -> null` (`src/test/java/spark/resource/UriPathTest.java`).
4) After lookup, Spark enforces a root check: `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstInClassPath(resource.getPath(), baseResource);` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java`, lines 82-84). That check throws if the resolved classpath resource is not under the configured base folder (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java`, lines 12-15, 30-33).
5) The project already has a regression test showing traversal is blocked in practice: `StaticFilesTest.testDirectoryTraversalProtectionLocal` sends an encoded `..\\spark\\Spark.class` path and expects HTTP 400 (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTest.java`, lines 141-145).
response.new_reason:: The report's null-byte theory is speculative. There is no evidence in this repository that the code was intended to be `path.contains("\0")`, and no demonstrated source-to-sink path showing that a NUL in a classpath resource name causes traversal through `ClassLoader.getResourceAsStream()` in Spark's actual static-file serving flow.

response.analysis: The sink is real in isolation: `LocalFolderImpl.resolve()` delegates to `VFSManager.resolveFile()`, and that helper does not normalize or reject `..`. However, the report's claimed source is not raw attacker-controlled traversal input in the actual OpenOLAT request flow. `FolderMapper` is only exposed through the generic mapper framework, and all `/m/` mapper requests pass through `MapperDispatcher.execute()`, which rejects any mapper subpath containing `..` with HTTP 403 before `FolderMapper.handle()` is ever called. As a result, the concrete path claimed by the report (`user request -> FolderMapper.handle(relPath) -> container.resolve(relPath) -> VFSManager.resolveFile()`) is not exploitable for CWE-022 in normal product operation. This is therefore a false positive for the reported real-world vulnerability, even though the utility method would be worth hardening defensively.
response.evidence: `FolderMapper.handle()` directly calls `container.resolve(relPath)` in `src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/modules/bc/FolderMapper.java:45-48`. `LocalFolderImpl.resolve()` forwards to `VFSManager.resolveFile()` in `src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/LocalFolderImpl.java:469-476`, and `VFSManager.resolveFile()` indeed uses `new File(base, childName)` without rejecting `..` (`VFSManager.java:101-109` and `170-201`). But `FolderMapper` is only instantiated in `src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/modules/bc/FolderRunController.java:595` via `registerMapper(ureq, new FolderMapper(...))`. `BasicController.registerMapper()` uses the mapper service to generate a `/m/{id}` URL, and `MapperServiceImpl.register()` sets the URL under `DispatcherModule.PATH_MAPPED` (`/m/`). Every such request is handled by `src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/mapper/MapperDispatcher.java:73-112`, which computes the subpath `mod` and blocks it with `if (mod.indexOf("..") != -1) { ... 403 ...; return; }` at lines 105-109 before calling `m.handle(mod, hreq)`. Upstream path extraction in `DispatcherModule.subtractContextPath()` also URL-decodes the request URI when needed (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/DispatcherModule.java:107-130`). Therefore the reported attacker-controlled `relPath` containing `..` does not reach `FolderMapper` in the real HTTP flow.

#### 3. CVE-2021-21345 — CORBA ServerTableEntry Gadget (CWE-078)
**File:** `xstream-distribution/src/content/CVE-2021-21345.html`
**Mechanism:** A deeply nested gadget chain routes through `com.sun.corba.se.impl.activation.ServerTableEntry`, whose `activationCmd` field holds the OS command string. During deserialization, the chain eventually calls the entry's `verify()` method, which executes the command via `Runtime.exec()`.
```xml
<jaxbObject class='com.sun.corba.se.impl.activation.ServerTableEntry'>
<activationCmd>calc</activationCmd>
</jaxbObject>
```
**Affected versions:** All versions ≤ 1.4.15 used without a whitelist.

response.analysis: The report describes a real historical XStream gadget chain, but as a finding against this repository/branch it is a false positive. The source would be attacker-controlled XML passed into XStream deserialization, and the sink would be command execution inside com.sun.corba.se.impl.activation.ServerTableEntry.verify(). That sink is real for old vulnerable configurations, but in this codebase the default deserialization path installs a SecurityMapper and a default allowlist before unmarshalling. As a result, the classes used by the CVE-2021-21345 chain are rejected before object creation, so the chain cannot reach Runtime.exec() in the current shipped code. The cited file is also a documentation/advisory page that explicitly says only versions up to 1.4.15 were affected out of the box, while this repository is 1.5.0-SNAPSHOT. Therefore this is not a real CWE-078 issue in the current project unless a downstream application explicitly disables XStream's default security or manually re-allows dangerous gadget types.
response.evidence: pom.xml:17 shows this repository is version 1.5.0-SNAPSHOT. The cited advisory page xstream-distribution/src/content/CVE-2021-21345.html:23-25 states that only versions <= 1.4.15 are affected out of the box and that users using a whitelist are not affected. In xstream/src/java/com/thoughtworks/xstream/XStream.java:633-637, each XStream instance runs setupMappers() and setupSecurity() during construction. In XStream.java:665, the mapper chain includes new SecurityMapper(mapper). In XStream.java:721-777, setupSecurity() installs default permissions/allowlists. In xstream/src/java/com/thoughtworks/xstream/mapper/SecurityMapper.java:72-91, realClass() resolves each class and immediately calls checkPermissions(type), throwing ForbiddenClassException if the type is not allowed. In xstream/src/java/com/thoughtworks/xstream/security/ExplicitTypePermission.java:49-52, allowTypes only permits exact class names, so the presence of Object.class in the default allowed set does not whitelist all subclasses. The changelog at xstream-distribution/src/content/changes.html:361-374 further states that com.sun.corba.se.impl.activation.ServerTableEntry and related gadget types are blacklisted by default. Therefore attacker-controlled XML containing ServerTableEntry or the required intermediary gadget types is blocked before the gadget chain can execute.

response.analysis: The reported CWE-772 path is not valid. The resource at issue is the JDBC connection created by `beginTransaction(...)`. In both `runInTransaction` overloads, execution always ends by calling `connection.commit()` on success or `connection.rollback()` in the catch path. Those no-arg methods delegate to `commit(true)` and `rollback(true)`, and both of those methods unconditionally call `closeJdbcConnection()` from their own `finally` blocks. Because the close is already guaranteed inside the callee, the lack of an extra outer `finally` in `runInTransaction` does not create a leak. The report's claim that `commit()` may throw before the close runs is inconsistent with the actual Java control flow here.
response.evidence: Cleanup on transaction-setup failure is already present in `Sql2o.beginTransaction(ConnectionSource,int)` at `core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Sql2o.java:297-305`: `finally { if (!success) { connection.close(); } }`. Cleanup on rollback failure is already present in `core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Connection.java:138-147`: `try { jdbcConnection.rollback(); } catch (SQLException e) { ... } finally { if(closeConnection) this.closeJdbcConnection(); }`. Cleanup on commit failure is already present in `core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Connection.java:155-165`: `try { jdbcConnection.commit(); } catch (SQLException e) { throw new Sql2oException(e); } finally { if(closeConnection) this.closeJdbcConnection(); }`. The actual close helper at `core/src/main/java/org/sql2o/Connection.java:324-331` catches `SQLException` from `jdbcConnection.close()`, so it is still invoked even when commit/rollback fail. Therefore the alleged path `runInTransaction -> commit()/rollback() exception -> leaked connection` is not a reachable resource-leak path in this code.

response.analysis: This is a false positive. The reported CWE-772 path does not produce a real worker leak in RxJava's actual lifecycle. The key mistake is that the report overlooks an existing release path: `AbstractWindowObserver` initializes `windowCount` to `1`, so an immediate downstream cancellation at the earliest reachable point causes `windowDone()` to decrement `1 -> 0`, which invokes `cleanupResources()` and disposes the `Worker` before `createFirstWindow()` returns. The report's statement that cleanup requires a prior increment in `createFirstWindow()` is incorrect. In addition, the report assumes `downstreamCancelled` can become true before `downstream.onSubscribe(this)`, but external code does not hold the operator's `Disposable` until that callback, so the claimed control-flow race is misstated.
response.evidence: Code path: `AbstractWindowObserver` constructor sets `windowCount = new AtomicInteger(1)`; `onSubscribe()` stores `upstream`, then calls `downstream.onSubscribe(this)`, then `createFirstWindow()`. If the downstream disposes immediately from `onSubscribe`, `dispose()` calls `windowDone()`, and `windowDone()` executes `cleanupResources()` when `windowCount.decrementAndGet() == 0`. For `WindowSkipObserver`, `cleanupResources()` is `worker.dispose()`. Therefore the earliest reachable cancel path is: `downstream.onSubscribe(this)` -> `dispose()` -> `windowDone()` -> `cleanupResources()` -> `worker.dispose()` -> return to `createFirstWindow()`, which now sees `downstreamCancelled` and skips scheduling. No worker remains undisposed. Supporting test behavior exists in `ObservableWindowWithTimeTest`, which calls `TestHelper.checkDisposed(...)` for `window(2, 1, TimeUnit.DAYS, Schedulers.single())`; `TestHelper.checkDisposed(Observable<?>)` disposes the subscription immediately inside `Observer.onSubscribe`, i.e. exactly the 'cancel before first window work starts' scenario.

response.analysis: The underlying CVE is historically real for old XStream releases, but this report is a false positive for the current repository. The reported file `xstream-distribution/src/content/CVE-2020-26217.html` is an advisory/documentation page, not executable implementation code. In the current checkout, `XStream` installs a default whitelist during construction, and non-whitelisted classes are rejected before deserialization can instantiate gadget types. As a result, attacker-controlled XML does not reach the `javax.imageio.spi.FilterIterator` / `javax.imageio.ImageIO$ContainsFilter` / `java.lang.ProcessBuilder.start()` gadget chain in the default product configuration. A CWE-078 path would only exist if an application explicitly weakened XStream security by broad allowlisting or equivalent misconfiguration, which is outside the default code path of this repo.
response.evidence: 1) The report points to `xstream-distribution/src/content/CVE-2020-26217.html`, which is a historical CVE write-up rather than a sink in implementation code. 2) That page itself states only versions `<= 1.4.13` are affected, and users using the whitelist are not affected (`CVE-2020-26217.html:23-25`). 3) The current repo version is `1.5.0-SNAPSHOT` (`pom.xml:14`). 4) `XStream` calls `setupSecurity()` in its constructor and installs default allow-permissions (`xstream/src/java/com/thoughtworks/xstream/XStream.java:716-777`). 5) `SecurityMapper.realClass()` immediately calls `checkPermissions(type)` and throws `ForbiddenClassException` for types not allowed by policy (`xstream/src/java/com/thoughtworks/xstream/mapper/SecurityMapper.java:71-91`). 6) The regression test for this gadget only works after explicit opt-in: `xstream.allowTypes("javax.imageio.ImageIO$ContainsFilter"); xstream.allowTypeHierarchy(Iterator.class);` before `xstream.fromXML(xml)` (`xstream/src/test/com/thoughtworks/acceptance/SecurityVulnerabilityTest.java:109-137`). This demonstrates the gadget is not reachable by default in the current codebase.

#### 2. CVE-2020-26217 — ImageIO$ContainsFilter + ProcessBuilder Gadget (CWE-078)
**File:** `xstream-distribution/src/content/CVE-2020-26217.html`
**Mechanism:** A complex gadget chain using `javax.imageio.spi.FilterIterator` + `javax.imageio.ImageIO$ContainsFilter` invokes `ProcessBuilder.start()` reflectively via a `java.lang.reflect.Method` object deserialized from the XML. <command><string>calc</string></command>
